home.manifestoQuote: We are not prophets. We are designers, researchers and activists who believe in the power of the right questions, open tools and informed communities.

finalCta.title: The future is built together
finalCta.description: Relatronica is an independent, open-source project. If you share this vision, join the community.

about.title: Relatronica
about.tagline: We imagine possible futures and create tools that help communities understand, discuss and shape them, empowering civic participation on fundamental issues for our collective future.

mission.title: Our Mission
mission.description: Relatronica is a design studio born in Switzerland in 2024 from a team of designers and researchers who met at CERN, the European Organization for Nuclear Research. It combines {speculativeDesign}, {civicTech} and {knowledgeMapping} to explore future scenarios and create tools that help communities understand, discuss and navigate the complexities of the contemporary world, fostering informed participation in decisions affecting humanity.
mission.speculativeDesignTooltip: A creative approach that imagines possible future scenarios to stimulate critical reflections on how today's technologies and choices might shape tomorrow.
mission.civicTechTooltip: Digital technologies created to improve community life, facilitate civic participation and make public information more accessible to everyone.
mission.knowledgeMappingTooltip: The visualization of connections between ideas, concepts and data to make knowledge more understandable and help identify relationships that would otherwise remain hidden.

origins.title: Our Origins at CERN
origins.paragraph1: Relatronica was born in Switzerland in 2024 from the meeting, at CERN, between designers and researchers who share the same question: how can we use technologies and data to truly engage people in choices concerning humanity's future?
origins.paragraph2: CERN is one of the symbols of international scientific collaboration. It was here, in 1991, that the World Wide Web was born, conceived as an open space to share knowledge and make information accessible to everyone. Since then, the Web has revolutionized our lives, but many crucial decisions – on climate, energy, research, infrastructure – are still perceived as distant, technical, poorly participated.
origins.paragraph3: With Relatronica we want to carry forward and expand that original spirit: not just a Web where you read and inform yourself, but spaces and tools where people can understand, discuss and contribute. We transform complex topics into clear paths of participation, to bring citizens, institutions and scientific communities closer together.

speculativeDesign.title: Speculative Design
speculativeDesign.description: We design alternative future scenarios to stimulate critical thinking and open conversations about possible futures. Through prototypes and narratives, we explore the ethical, social and technological implications of possible developments.

civicTech.title: Civic Tech
civicTech.description: We develop digital technologies that empower communities and improve access to public information. We create tools that facilitate civic participation and democratic transparency.

knowledgeMapping.title: Knowledge Mapping
knowledgeMapping.description: We visualize relationships between concepts, data and ideas to make knowledge more accessible and understandable. Our mapping tools help navigate complexity and identify hidden connections.

approach.title: Our Approach
approach.intro: We combine research, design and technology to create projects that:

points.critical: Stimulate critical thinking about possible futures
points.ethics: Explore the ethical implications of emerging technologies

activism.title: Activism for Responsible Technologies
activism.intro: We firmly believe that technology must be designed and developed responsibly, ethically and oriented towards collective well-being. Our commitment goes beyond design: we are active in promoting a more aware and critical technological culture.

ethics.title: Ethics and Humanity
ethics.description: We promote technologies that put people at the center, respect human rights and foster inclusion and social equity.

transparency.title: Transparency and Control
transparency.description: We support transparent, open source and decentralized systems that restore control and autonomy to communities and individuals.

education.title: Critical Education
education.description: We create tools and resources that help understand the implications of emerging technologies and develop informed critical thinking.

sustainability.title: Digital Sustainability
sustainability.description: We design solutions that consider environmental impact, energy efficiency and the longevity of digital systems.

values.title: Our Values
values.intro: The principles that guide our work and our design choices:

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.description: We believe that information and tools must be accessible to everyone, regardless of technical background or available resources.

participation.title: Participation
participation.description: We promote collaborative and inclusive processes that involve communities in defining their own future.

critical.title: Critical Thinking
critical.description: We stimulate deep reflections and uncomfortable questions, avoiding simplistic solutions to complex problems.

responsibility.title: Responsibility
responsibility.description: We take responsibility for the ethical, social and environmental implications of our creations.

openness.title: Openness
openness.description: We favor open source, transparent and reproducible approaches that promote knowledge sharing.

nexthuman.title: NextHuman
nexthuman.paragraph1: This project represents an example of our work: a calendar of probabilistic deadlines for humanity. Through interactive visualizations and speculative scenarios, we explore the challenges and opportunities awaiting our collective future, combining knowledge mapping to show relationships between events and civic tech to make this information accessible to everyone.
nexthuman.paragraph2: NextHuman is also a manifesto of our approach: we use technology to stimulate awareness, foster public debate and encourage concrete actions towards more desirable futures. Every visualization, every scenario, every mapped connection is an invitation to reflect, discuss and act responsibly.

collaborate.title: Want to Collaborate?
collaborate.description: We are always interested in collaborations, projects and conversations about speculative design, civic tech, knowledge mapping and responsible technologies. If you share our values and want to explore possible futures together, don't hesitate to contact us.

projects.title: Projects
projects.subtitle: Explorations at the intersection of speculative design, civic tech and knowledge mapping

nexthuman.title: NextHuman
nexthuman.subtitle: Calendar of Deadlines for Humanity
nexthuman.description: An interactive platform that visualizes the probabilistic deadlines awaiting humanity, combining knowledge mapping, speculative scenarios and temporal visualizations.

segno.title: SEGNO
segno.subtitle: Digital sovereignty
segno.description: A platform to take back control of your digital life. It offers learning paths on privacy, digital rights and GDPR, practical tools for data protection, a map of your digital footprint, and an ethical stack to evaluate the sovereignty of the services we use every day.

rebica.title: Rebica
rebica.subtitle: SDL Citizen Lab
rebica.description: Turns future scenarios into executable code. Instead of static slides, every hypothesis is simulated with thousands of Monte Carlo runs on real data and cited sources. Interactive parameters, verifiable results, all in the browser without sending any data. Scenarios on AI and employment, energy consumption, drug discovery, digital euro.

civica.title: Civica
civica.subtitle: Political systems configurator
civica.description: An interactive platform to explore, compare and configure different political systems. Allows you to understand the implications of institutional choices through visualizations and comparative scenarios.

neuralforming.title: Neuralforming
neuralforming.subtitle: Hybrid board game for schools
neuralforming.description: Transform your classroom into a democratic parliament. Neuralforming is a hybrid board game that engages students in an immersive AI governance experience: they propose laws, debate ethical implications and vote on decisions that will shape the future of artificial intelligence. An educational experience that combines play, democracy and technological awareness.

argo.title: Progetto Argo
argo.subtitle: Privacy-first note-taking app
argo.description: A note-taking application based on end-to-end encryption, zero knowledge architecture and privacy by design. Designed to ensure your notes remain private and under your exclusive control, without compromises on security or user experience.

eclipse.title: Eclipse
eclipse.subtitle: Privacy-first menstrual cycle tracking
eclipse.description: An app that restores sovereignty over your body's data. In a landscape where menstrual tracking apps sell intimate information to third parties, Eclipse puts privacy at the center: end-to-end encryption, zero knowledge architecture, and data that stays exclusively on your device. A digital act of resistance for bodily autonomy and reproductive health, because your cycle data is not a commodity to be sold.

mauss.title: Mauss
mauss.subtitle: Volunteer computing for research
mauss.description: A volunteer computing network inspired by the gift: participants donate unused device cycles to open research projects. No centralized data centers, no access barriers — just a colony of nodes that together put computational power at the service of public science.

projects.noResults: No projects match the selected filters.

contact.title: Have a project in mind?
contact.description: We are always interested in collaborating on projects that explore the future through speculative design, civic tech and knowledge mapping.

modal.description: NextHuman is an interactive platform that visualizes the {modalAction1} awaiting humanity. It is not a prediction, but a {modalAction2} exercise that combines knowledge mapping, future scenarios and temporal visualizations to help understand the complexities of tomorrow.

deadlines.title: Probabilistic Deadlines
deadlines.description: Future events with probability of occurrence based on analysis and trends

mapping.title: Knowledge Mapping
mapping.description: Visualization of connections between events, themes and scenarios

scenarios.title: Speculative Scenarios
scenarios.description: Exploration of possible futures through interactive visualizations

explore.title: Explore the Board
explore.description: Navigate freely in the interactive whiteboard. Each card represents a probabilistic deadline with date, probability and associated themes.

filters.title: Use Filters
filters.description: Filter by {themes} (climate, technology, geopolitics...) or {probabilities} to focus on specific areas of interest.

scenarios.title: Explore Scenarios
scenarios.description: Select between {optimistic}, {realistic} or {pessimistic} scenarios to see how event probabilities change.

connections.title: Discover Connections
connections.description: Click on an event to see details, implications and links to in-depth resources. Connections between events show how the future is interconnected.

importantNote.title: Important Note
importantNote.description: NextHuman is not a scientific prediction, but a {speculative} tool to stimulate critical reflections on possible futures. Probabilities are indicative and based on trend analysis, not rigorous predictive models.

inspector.actionsDescription: Discover concrete actions you can take to prepare for or contribute to this future event

climate.title: Climate & Environment
climate.description: Climate tipping points and global environmental crises.

energy.title: Energy & Resources
energy.description: Energy transition and natural resource management.

technology.title: Technology & Science
technology.description: AI, scientific research and disruptive technological innovations.

society.title: Society & Demographics
society.description: Social, demographic transformations and health aspects.

governance.title: Economy & Geopolitics
governance.description: Global economy, geopolitics and change governance.

1.title: First nearly ice-free Arctic summer
1.description: Arctic September sea ice may fall below 1 million km² (practically ice-free). This is not a classic irreversible tipping point: ice-free summers can recur with increasing frequency depending on emissions.

1.title: Support climate policies
1.description: Vote for and support politicians who advance climate action

2.title: AGI / human-level AI
2.description: Around a 50% chance that AI systems reach human-comparable capabilities across a wide range of cognitive tasks. Expert and prediction-market estimates have shifted toward 2030–2035 versus longer timelines from earlier years.

3.title: World population reaches its peak
3.description: According to the UN World Population Prospects 2024, world population is expected to peak around 10.3 billion in the mid-2080s, then gradually decline.

4.title: Advanced renewable energy transition
4.description: Possible completion of a global energy transition in which renewables dominate the power mix and fossil fuels enter a structural demand decline. IEA outlooks show rapid progress still insufficient for Net Zero 2050 without policy acceleration.

5.title: Collapse of a major marine ecosystem
5.description: Risk of functional collapse of a critical marine ecosystem (coral reef, kelp forests or key coastal systems), with cascading effects on fisheries, tourism and biodiversity.

6.title: Global water crisis
6.description: Possible global water crisis with billions of people living in areas of severe or chronic water scarcity, worsened by drought, intensive agriculture and climate stress.

7.title: Technological singularity
7.description: Speculative scenario in which technological progress (driven by recursively improving AI) becomes so rapid that it is hard for human institutions to understand or govern.

8.title: Global geopolitical reorganization
8.description: Possible significant reorganization of world geopolitical balances, with new power blocs, technology rivalries and redefined alliances and international institutions.

9.title: Severe global biodiversity loss
9.description: Risk of acceleration of the sixth mass extinction, with substantial loss (up to roughly 30–50% under inaction scenarios) of species and ecosystem function if conservation is not strengthened.

10.title: Global debt crisis
10.description: Possible systemic sovereign and private debt crisis that could destabilize the world economy, especially amid high rates, climate shocks and geopolitical fragmentation.

11.title: Earth Overshoot Day 2026
11.description: The day humanity has used all the ecological resources Earth can regenerate in a year. In 2026 it falls on July 30. It is an annual indicator: the date shifts each year with footprint and biocapacity.

12.title: Longevity Escape Velocity
12.description: According to radical longevity scenarios (e.g. Ray Kurzweil), we will gain more than one year of life expectancy per year lived through medicine, biotech and AI. Remains highly speculative.

13.title: Fit for 55: −55% EU emissions
13.description: Binding EU target to cut net greenhouse gas emissions by at least 55% vs 1990 by 2030 (not climate neutrality, which is set for 2050). Implemented via the Fit for 55 package.

14.title: First human mission to Mars
14.description: Possible launch or arrival of the first human Mars mission, marking humanity's expansion beyond Earth. Timelines depend on NASA, ESA, SpaceX and technical/financial constraints.

15.title: Practical quantum advantage
15.description: Quantum computers may demonstrate useful advantages on practical problems (cryptography, materials, optimization, pharma) beyond lab benchmarks.

17.title: Global Net Zero 2050 target
17.description: Global policy target for net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by mid-century, necessary (but increasingly difficult) to keep warming near 1.5°C. IEA/IPCC pathways require marked acceleration.

18.title: AMOC collapse or tipping
18.description: Risk of critical slowdown or collapse of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC), with potential drastic climate shifts in Europe and the North Atlantic. Estimates diverge: some observational studies suggest mid-century; others judge a full 21st-century collapse unlikely.

19.title: Structural decline of the oil era
19.description: Possible end of oil as the dominant pillar of the global energy system, with demand peak followed by structural decline. Distinct from classic production 'peak oil'.

20.title: Global urbanization at 70%
20.description: About 70% of the world's population will live in urban areas, with major consequences for infrastructure, resources, health and inequality.

22.title: Disappearance of Alpine glaciers
22.description: Alpine glaciers may shrink drastically or nearly disappear at mid elevations, with impacts on water supply, tourism and hydrogeological risk in Europe.

23.title: Global food crisis
23.description: Possible severe global or regional food crisis due to climate change, water scarcity, geopolitical shocks and demographic pressure.

24.title: Commercial nuclear fusion
24.description: Possible achievement of commercially sustainable nuclear fusion, with plants delivering net energy economically. ITER and private startups pursue demos, but commercial scale remains uncertain.

26.title: Global population aging
26.description: For the first time in history, globally people over 65 will outnumber children under 5, with deep implications for welfare, labour, health systems and growth.

27.title: End of coal as a primary energy source
27.description: Possible structural exit of coal from a primary energy role in major markets, with accelerated OECD phase-out and demand peaks in Asia. Pathways remain uneven by country.

28.title: Global fisheries collapse
28.description: Risk of collapse of key global fish stocks unless sustainable fishing, marine protected areas and international governance are strengthened.

29.title: SDG 2030 deadline
29.description: Formal deadline for the UN Sustainable Development Goals. Many targets are already off track; the horizon remains a global political stocktake moment.

30.title: Climate refugee crisis
30.description: Possible large-scale migration crisis with tens or hundreds of millions displaced by extremes, sea-level rise, aridification and loss of livelihoods.

0.title: Support refugee organizations
0.description: Contribute to organizations helping climate refugees

1.title: Promote welcoming policies
1.description: Support policies that address climate migration

31.title: EU 2040 climate target (−90%)
31.description: Binding EU intermediate target to cut net greenhouse gas emissions by 90% vs 1990 by 2040, on the path to climate neutrality by 2050 (updated European Climate Law).

32.title: 1.5°C breach (multi-year mean)
32.description: Possible confirmation that mean global warming has sustainably exceeded 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels (multi-year average, not a single record year). Implies a nearly exhausted remaining carbon budget.

33.title: Operational AI governance (EU AI Act)
33.description: Operational entry into force of key EU AI Act obligations for high-risk systems and general-purpose models, with enforcement, transparency duties and AI risk governance in the EU market.

34.title: Amazon tipping / dieback risk
34.description: Risk that parts of the Amazon rainforest cross dieback thresholds (transition toward savanna or degraded ecosystems), with carbon releases and continental-scale biodiversity loss.

35.title: West Antarctic Ice Sheet instability
35.description: Risk of irreversible destabilization of sectors of the West Antarctic Ice Sheet (WAIS), with multi-metre sea-level contribution over long timescales. Triggering may occur this century even if full effects unfold later.

36.title: Global oil demand peak
36.description: According to IEA scenarios, global oil demand may peak then plateau or decline between the late 2020s and mid-2030s, driven by transport electrification, efficiency and climate policy.
